I think the Open Claw agent revolution is here. All right, let's get to Open Claw because I have been, what's the right term here? I think it's claw-pilled. I've been claw-pilled. The company has settled on the name Open Claw, or I should say the open source project is Open Claw. What is Open Claw? It lets you create an agent, a personality, we call them Replicants here at my investment firm Launch and at This Week in Startups. And then you can give Open Claw access to things like your calendar, your Gmail, your Notion instance, your Slack, your Twitter handle, your Reddit login, your LinkedIn login. And then it is designed to appease you and do multi-stage tasks like, hey, go and get my DMs from these three platforms and organize them, or find out from my email every morning and my Slack and create like a dossier of the day. These are very simple examples, but it's multi-stage examples and it can take actions on your behalf. Why is this such a huge revelation today, Alex? It's basically because all the other software programs that we've had have been sandboxed. All the AI products have been sandboxed. You can't go to ChatGPT and say, put this on my schedule. Certainly can't say, go find my password for Gmail, find all the travel receipts I have, and then make a Google Doc out of them or Google Sheet, and then cross-reference them with this. You just, you would be unwise for a company that's getting paid money that has liability to give the keys of the kingdom to an agent. However, you, as a sovereign individual, can be a lunatic and let AI have the keys to the kingdom. This is giving the teenagers the keys to the car and a credit card and the bank account and the Robinhood account. They can go trade, they can do anything. Yes, so now that we've gotten that out of the way, what do we have on deck today? I know we have two great guests, I know we've got some news, take me through what we're going to do.

full_transcript.text: Yeah, we're going to talk about Open Claw sites, what's called Vision Claw, a great way people are taking Open Claw into the real world. full_transcript.text: But first, I think it's time for a video demo of what happens when you take Meta's Ray-Ban glasses, the smart glasses, and sync them up to your AI. It's pretty cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: Good to be here, guys. Good to see Jason, Alex. Happy to be here.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Okay, and you have gone down the rabbit hole. You are too Claude-pilled, yeah?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: This is the first time I think that we have an application of AI that actually matches the vision of what humanity thought artificial intelligence would be in all the sci-fi books and movies leading up to today. full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: And that vision for open Claude is that the agent, we call them replicants, will go out in the world and do things that are productive for you, not just give you information, but go do tasks.

full_transcript.text: Yeah, my first thoughts are I don't find that remotely interesting, just to be quite honest with you. full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So you could see this getting a little more interesting, or in your example Alex, the person who's working at uh you know 7-Eleven just walking through the store looking at the items and then it making a decision as to what's the inventory. Alex Finn, your thoughts?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: What excites me and and when I try to think about it is is like I think we will hit a time in the very short future where open claw reaches the corporation level. Right now I think it's tinkerers, hobbyists, tech enthusiasts like you guys that have discovered this that have seen the magic. There's too many risks, there's too many security issues. And I'm scared that when that does happen it will lead to layoffs and job losses. And so what I've been trying to kind of tackle with everything I've been doing the last couple weeks and what I'm doing with all the hardware is like how can we figure out workflows that counteract that, counter balance that where solopreneurs like me, people working by themselves who don't have a job can create large economic impact, right? have...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: Basically entire companies of AI employees working for them. full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Can I double-click on that? To me though, if we give corporations increasingly powerful tools, I don't see where individuals will fit into that economic picture and have impact if we're granting already powerful organizations even more capacity, capability, and fluidity. full_transcript.text: Well, I'll give you an example, right? The way I think about it is I can do many more things at once. So one example, there's this big controversy over the last year. Here's the way I'm trying to change the framing though, is look at what I'm able to do with this technology. I'm able to build a business and have people work for me. So you, the Call of Duty artists, can now go and instead of creating art for Call of Duty, you can build your own video games, you build your own Call of Duty. For me, I'm building my own content company with all these tools, with all these AI agents that work for me. full_transcript.text: Yeah, so my vision for the future of AI is everyone having their own personal local intelligence working for them 24/7. I have a SaaS I built by myself with AI called CreatorBuddy, which is AI social tools. I create tons of content, I have a YouTube channel with 100k subscribers. The higher-up managers, the strategic advisors, are cloud-based so they don't have to do all of the work. And then the lower-level agents, the ones that are actually doing work 24/7, are all local on my computer running on GLM 4 7B. And what you can see here is many things are going on. So they're constantly working, but what will happen throughout the day, I set up this demo mode so I can show this to you in real time, what will happen throughout the day is many things. full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn

And so, Alex, Matt, I'm just curious on the skill front if you think this is a, a good enough step to get people to a point of comfort.

full_transcript.speaker: Matt Van Horn
full_transcript.text: Confidence or if this is just a thin Band-Aid over a gaping wound and therefore not enough to really solve any of the security issues we've talked about.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: I think it's again, it's a Band-Aid, right? Like I have the most popular X search tool, like why should I be allowed to do that? Like the X company should be able to stop me. Like who do I-- I literally, I never looked at the code. I trust it because I gave it very simple instructions, right? But I own that skill, right? And so like, and it's-- it says X on it. It doesn't use like, you remember like when you had to write a Twitter app back in the day, it had to be called Tweetie or something like that, right? And so like, it's good that someone is looking at my code to confirm that it's not malware at least. I think not malware, doesn't work, like these are the basic tests that are important. And then verification can come later.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I just-- I just gave my team the green light to use the X API and pay, I think, 100 or 200 bucks a month for the basic one. What's the difference between like, are you using MCP or scraping, how does the-- or the Claude sidebar, Chrome extension, how are you with last 30 days getting X content versus using the API?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so back in the day when I built last 30 days, less than 30 days ago, there was no X API that was available to the public. So I was using the X AI API, if that makes sense, which-- which-- which fairly charges you per token. I've not done the math between the new X API that launched one week ago versus the X AI, but I think that it just is more personalized for you and your account and being able to post, etc. I believe on the X front, but-- but again, the core mechanics of the Bird CLI, you can still do it for free. So that still exists. It's deleted from GitHub, but ask your agent to figure out how to do it and your agent will figure out it. It is not blocked by X yet. By the way, I did get the response on the Opus from last 30 days, so the biggest thing is context window. So million token context window up from 200k. So that's the biggest win. And then what Alex mentioned, the agent teams, is the real differentiators. And yeah, people-- people like it.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: There's also fast mode for 4o, which costs several times as much, but you can kind of jump to the top of the queue and get faster inference. I'm really curious to see who's going to pay for that because it was already not cheap, Matt, and paying a multiple of that per token, even you with your venture money will eventually start to cry, I think, at the amount of money you'll spend there.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Alright, absolute fantastic. Let's drop the boys off. Matt, great job. Ping me about the investment. Put in a little frisky bet. Oh, I'm feeling frisky. Open Claude's got me feeling frisky. I got the checkbook right here. I gotta wire some-- I gotta send some wires. Alex Finn, if you want to be an entrepreneur at some point and like take venture money, let's talk, you and I. You have no outside investors, right, Alex?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: Uh, well, to cover the first part, I'm already an entrepreneur, but the second part of taking investment...

full_transcript.speaker: Matt Van Horn
full_transcript.text: money, have not done that yet, but you'll be the first person I go to, Jason.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, there we go. I always like to be the first or third or fourth investor in great entrepreneurs and their companies. I also have the domain name begin.com, b-i-g-i-n dot com, which I yes, spent hundreds of thousands of dollars on and I think that is the best name possible for an open claw software product. So—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Not ai.com?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I mean, I did there was an ai.com commercial. Let's play it and get everybody's reaction. Go ahead, Alex. Play the open— This is the ai.com. Some crypto company bought ai.com for $70 million. And I had a two-letter domain name 20.com and I bought it for 70k 15 years ago. I sold it five years later for 1.8 million or something. So, uh, I probably should have held on to that, but ai.com, whoever owned that, right place, right time. Here's the 30-second commercial. Go.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: If you're on the audio version, it's two balls flying through the air. They collide and they form an ai.com logo that looks very generic like some old TWA logo. It says AGI is coming and then it prompts you to reserve your username. Jason, I did this. I'm now ai.com/alexwilhelm. Uh, but I will say that the scuttlebutt around the internet is that ai.com is essentially a Open Claw clone. So the idea here is that the guys behind crypto.com, great branding, great domain name, are now going to build something called ai.com. It's promising an agent that'll do actual work for you. They spent a lot of money on the domain and the Super Bowl commercial and uh, I Godspeed. Oliver, your thoughts?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, the Super Bowl commercial was not great, obviously. It was— they probably spent— they could have spent $20 in in Hayscale credits on that video.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: They probably— that's probably the case, by the way. That looked like they spent $20.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: I mean, if you're watching the Super Bowl and it's loud and that epic music comes on, you'll get its attention. I I do believe that they had the— you can reserve a domain name. That was kind of the whole hook of the commercial is that people were rushing over to reserve a domain name. You can— I think producer Marcus got Marcus B. I was trying to get some low amount of letters domain name. It then the next step after you reserve your domain name was to put your credit card in. So that step I just— that's where I logged out.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: That's what I was like, nah, I'm good. I don't— the crypto.com guys, I don't know if I want them to have my— my good credit card. Pass.

full_transcript.speaker: Matt Van Horn
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I have a rule that whenever someone advertises their product but does not talk about what the product is or what the value is of the product, that I just automatically assume it's a scam and I do not buy into it. And I went to ai.com and the first thing it did was ask my credit card number without even telling me what the product was. And I said no way, there is a 0% chance this ever turns into anything. This is 100% a scam. I mean, I don't want to call this other company a scam, but you know, the ear—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Clearly, clearly advertising was very much the same or it was like cheat on everything but then it doesn't tell you what the product is or what the product does and it's like if you have a good product, I feel like you would tell me what it is. But the fact that you're not telling me what it is, I'm gonna pass.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You get to a credit card form and then you have to pay to reserve your name, but they don't charge your card. So I guess they're doing that to charge you in the future maybe.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: But at no point do they tell you what you're buying. You're buying a username, but for what? And they, they don't tell you what that is. There's no part of the onboarding that's like you're getting a username for an AI agent that will do this for you. It's you're buying AGI. Here, give me your credit card number.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: You know what? I'm going to take the other side of it. I like it. I don't know why I like it, uh, but I like that they have the audacity to do something so deranged as spend $70 million on an incredible domain name, give everybody a handle and figure it out later. I think what they should have just charged is charge $1 per handle and let you buy up to 10. That would be great. Or the first one's free, but and you get 10 of them, then just have you create the massive domain squatting. Let every credit card buy 10, every email. Uh, all right Matt, you uh are going to order some sushi on your Open Claw. Let's see it. What's going on over here? You order me lunch?

full_transcript.speaker: Matt Van Horn
full_transcript.text: So I don't have long till my next meeting, so I ordered one of the most powerful things I've built. Anyway, it's finally good, it works. I just ordered sushi. But here's something interesting when I set this up. So it cost $19 for my sushi order. I actually said back in the day, you know, a week ago when I set it up, do not spend more than $20 in case it like adds it four times or something, right? That was my intent. Anyway, it just said, logged in, going to order sushi, the total's 20.73 up from 19.58, the roll went up one dollar. That's over $20, you still want the order? And so I literally would not have noticed this ever, but my Open Claw caught that dollar they raised prices since Friday, which is crazy.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: My friend had sent me over an X post that I think was going viral that was talking about how Open Claw has a lot of security risks and it was an example where someone was trying to maximize their Open Claw and it bought an Alex Hormozi subscription, like that was like $2,000 and basically the person went into Open Claw and was said what have you done for the last 24 hours and explained how it bought these subscriptions to make your life better, to to help improve everything, to help Open Claw make your life better basically. But that kind of asks, brings me to my next question for Alex, which is you mentioned security was a concern for a lot of SMBs and enterprises and they shouldn't necessarily start using this tool now but they will in the future. Why would you say that they shouldn't start at the moment? Should they start tinkering with it? Because at launch we're seeing a lot of potential impact.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Finn
full_transcript.text: Oh no, if the messaging people understood from what I was saying is that companies should not tinker with it, that is not the message at all. My message to everyone is everyone should be tinkering with it. Every company, business, corporation, SMB should be tinkering with it. There are risks, there are security risks, right? Which is what makes it such a colossal opportunity at the moment is there are so many people scared. And when your competition is scared to use something, scared to dip their toes in something that's ultra-powerful, that's your time to strike. That's your time to be careful, to make sure you do it the right way, but you also need to make sure you figure out how to implement it and use it because that's where the opportunity is. I mean, a big reason why I think my content, my channel's blown up so much in the last two weeks is I haven't been scared to make the investment and go all the way in and experiment and do new things while other people are kind of scared to go out and buy a Mac Mini for $600, I bought $20,000 of Mac Studios, right? And so when your competition's nervous and scared to go all in, that's when you should be striking. So I think all the SMBs and corporations, if they're smart, would be implementing this and figuring out how to create value.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Uh, we're at a brave new world. Uh, great job, gentlemen. Matt Van Horn's dropped off, Alex will drop off as well, and I think we have one more partner to say thank you to. Alex, let's do that.

full_transcript.text: So today on the Polymarket front, we're going to ask a question, when does Claude 5 come out? Now, we talked a lot about Opus 3.5 Jason on the show today, everyone loves it. full_transcript.text: Well, what's most exciting about this is that the founder of—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, no.

highlights: Open Claw represents a shift from sandboxed AI to "agentic" technology that can access personal accounts like Gmail and Slack to execute complex, multi-stage tasks autonomously.
The "lights-out factory" model for software development is emerging, where AI agents write, run, and iterate on code without human intervention or manual review.
Professionals can hedge against AI-driven layoffs by automating their own roles and repositioning themselves as high-value orchestrators who manage teams of AI "replicants.".
Modern AI architecture is moving toward a hybrid model that uses elite cloud-based LLMs for strategic management while deploying local, private models for 24/7 repetitive labor.
Distribution remains the ultimate competitive advantage, as creators who build communities around their technical tinkering can quickly monetize SaaS products and private memberships.
summary:
This transcript from *This Week in Startups*, set in February 2026, centers on the "Open Claw agent revolution." Hosts Jason Calacanis and Alex Wilhelm, alongside guests Alex Finn and Matt Van Horn, discuss the transition from "sandboxed" AI to "Open Claw"—an open-source agentic technology that allows AI "replicants" to access personal data (Gmail, Slack, Notion) and perform autonomous, multi-stage tasks. The speakers argue that this shift represents a milestone as significant as the internet or mobile revolution, moving AI from a glorified search engine to a proactive worker capable of taking real-world actions on behalf of a "sovereign individual."

The discussion highlights several practical applications and demos, including "Vision Claw," which uses smart glasses to identify products and manage shopping carts, and "Last 30 Days," a tool for instant deep-dive research. A major theme is the rise of the "lights-out startup," where AI agents handle coding and operations without human intervention. Alex Finn demonstrates a virtual autonomous organization running on local hardware, where specialized agents (e.g., social media managers, engineers) collaborate 24/7. While the guests acknowledge security risks and the potential for corporate layoffs, they emphasize that this technology empowers solopreneurs and "middle managers" to automate their own jobs and increase their economic impact tenfold.

Overall, the transcript reflects a high-energy "Claw-pilled" sentiment, urging listeners to obsess over agentic AI to remain competitive. Key debates touch on the balance between using expensive cloud-based models (like Claude Opus) for reasoning and local, private models for routine labor. Calacanis concludes by announcing a dedicated investment fund for Open Claw startups, signaling a firm belief that the era of single-person, multi-agent companies has arrived and will fundamentally restructure the labor market and software development.
